data.title: Berkshire Hathaway's equity holdings will always exceed its cash pile, Buffett says
data.description:
Berkshire Hathaway's (NYSE:BRK.B) (NYSE:BRK.A) enviable cash and short-term Treasury holdings, now at a record $334.2B, have long been a focus by investors on potential uses for the stash. Some wonder why the company doesn't purchase more companies or stocks, buy back more of its shares, or pay a dividend.

Warren Buffett, who has become an investing legend by transforming a dying textiles business into an insurance and investing powerhouse, explained the company's priorities in using its capital in his annual letter to shareholders on Saturday. The majority of Berkshire investors' money is in equities. "That preference won't change" he said.

For the year, its ownership of marketable equities dropped from $354B to $272B, but the value of its non-quoted controlled equities "increased somewhat and remains far greater than the value of the marketable portfolio," Buffett said.

"Berkshire shareholders can rest assured that we will forever deploy a substantial majority of their money in equities – mostly American equities, although many of these will have international operations of significance," he wrote. "Berkshire will never prefer ownership of cash-equivalent assets over the ownership of good businesses, whether controlled or only partially owned."

The company's equity holdings include both those it directly controls, holding at least 80% of the business, which are not easily sold, and publicly traded securities, which are easier to acquire and divest.

Most of the directly controlled equities are 100%-owned by Berkshire (NYSE:BRK.B), and currently, it has 189 such subsidiaries, a collection "worth many hundreds of billions" of dollars, including "a few rare gems, many good-but-far-from-fabulous businesses and some laggards that have been disappointments," he said. None of them are a major drag, Buffett added.

Berkshire's (NYSE:BRK.B) partial-ownership holdings were valued at $272B at year-end, including about a dozen "very large and highly profitable businesses" such as Apple (AAPL), American Express (AXP), Coca-Cola (KO), and Moody's (MCO).

The advantage to marketable equities is that the company can change course more easily if it makes a mistake, he said. However, "Berkshire’s present size, it should be underscored, diminishes this valuable option. We can’t come and go on a dime," Buffett explained. It may take a year or more to establish or exit an investment. In addition, as a minority shareholder, the company can't change management or control how the business is managed.

Obviously, Berkshire (BRK.B) can control how its controlled companies manage their businesses, but has less flexibility in selling when it has made a mistake. "In reality, Berkshire almost never sells controlled businesses unless we face what we believe to be unending problems," Buffett wrote.

As he has before, he explained the company's policy of not paying dividends to shareholders, preferring to reinvest the money into the company. Berkshire (BRK.B) only paid a dividend once in the 60 years Buffett has controlled it. On Jan. 3, 1967, the company paid a $0.10 per A share (NYSE:BRK.A) dividend.

"For sixty years, Berkshire shareholders endorsed continuous reinvestment and that enabled the company to build its taxable income," he said.

Buffett also warned that keeping too much of the company's money in paper can be dangerous. "Paper money can see its value evaporate if fiscal folly prevails. In some countries, this reckless practice has become habitual, and, in our country’s short history, the U.S. has come close to the edge. Fixed-coupon bonds provide no protection against runaway currency." he wrote.

That brings him back to his belief in America: "The American process has not always been pretty – our country has forever had many scoundrels and promoters who seek to take advantage of those who mistakenly trust them with their savings. But even with such malfeasance – which remains in full force today – and also much deployment of capital that eventually floundered because of brutal competition or disruptive innovation, the savings of Americans has delivered a quantity and quality of output beyond the dreams of any colonist."

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Notable analyst calls this week: Airbnb, Gilead and Snowflake stocks among top picks
data.description:
The S&P500 (SP500) closed in the red on Friday, after the week saw dour economic reports, headlines on U.S. President Donald Trump’s tariff policy and corporate earnings from firms including Walmart (WMT).

For the week, Nasdaq (COMP:IND) lost 0.4%, while Dow (DJI) declined 0.9%.

Wall Street had a slew of upgrades and downgrades from analysts. Here are some of the major calls for the week:

Argus upgrades Airbnb on growth outlook

Argus upgraded Airbnb (NASDAQ:ABNB) to Buy from Hold, saying that it views the company as well positioned for growth within the online travel segment as the industry continues to gain momentum.

The brokerage, which set a PT of $190, expects 2025 revenue to increase 12% to $12.4B largely on increased international demand and higher room rates with bookings expected to continue increasing while at the same time, cancellations decline, and occupancy rates increase.

Analyst John Staszak pointed out that the company offers a “superior” travel experience.

Gilead upgraded by Deutsche, Merck receives downgrade

Deutsche Bank upgraded Gilead Sciences (NASDAQ:GILD) to Buy from Hold and raised PT to $120 from $80, citing the antiviral developer’s HIV treatment franchise.

Analyst James Shin, who raised estimates for GILD’s financials for 2029 and beyond, said the brokerage believes GILD’s pipeline of long-acting orals and injectables can combine for ~$19B of HIV treatment revenues by FY2035.

Meanwhile, Shin downgraded Merck (NYSE:MRK) to Hold from Buy and lowered PT to $105 from $128 per share, arguing the “real culprit” behind the stock’s selloff since the recent earnings print is Keytruda’s loss of exclusivity in 2028. “Despite MRK building out its pipeline, past precedence shows the Street is very skeptical when mega blockbusters face loss of exclusivity (LOE),” the analyst wrote.

Jefferies impressed by Roku’s platform revenue growth

Jefferies upgraded Roku (NASDAQ:ROKU) to Hold from Underperform, impressed by the company’s platform revenue growth of +25% in the fourth quarter.

"Between the Q4 outperformance, a Q1 guide +3% to Street, and an FY guide that does not imply a slowdown on an adjusted basis, it is clear to us that Roku has strengthened its position as an ad platform," the analysts said and hiked PT to $100 from $55 on the stock.

Datadog in focus after analysts’ downgrade

Datadog (NASDAQ:DDOG) was downgraded to Peer Perform from Outperform by Wolfe Research due to lower-than-expected 2025 guidance and the possibility of shrinking margins.

"While we continue to believe in the market leading products and long-term success, we see a more turbulent period around fundamentals and market dynamics which we believe will limit growth and upside," said Wolfe analyst Alex Zukin.

Wells Fargo also downgraded the stock to Equal Weight from Overweight in part to slower growth and higher spending on investments in 2025. The brokerage also lowered PT to $140 from $152 following the release of Datadog's fourth quarter.

Snowflake upgraded by Wolfe ahead of Q4 results

Snowflake (NYSE:SNOW) was upgraded to Outperform from Peer Perform by Wolfe Research ahead of the cloud-based data platform's fourth quarter financial results.

"The foundation of our conviction resides in the combination of our checks clearly pointing towards stabilizing, and even improving, consumption trends and the numbers in recent quarters backing that up," said Wolfe analyst Alex Zukin and set a PT of $235 on the stock.

TD Cowen reiterated their Buy rating on Snowflake and upped PT to $210 from $190, with analysts saying that recent partner checks were “constructive”.

Akamai’s quarterly results left Wall Street concerned

BofA downgraded Akamai (NASDAQ:AKAM) to Neutral and cut the price target to $100 from $125 on worries over the latest quarterly results and guidance.

“We see limited near term catalysts as the company transitions from slower growth areas like web security and legacy compute to high growth areas like API security and Cloud Infrastructure Services,” said analyst Madeline Brooks.

Needham kept its Hold rating and said expressed concern, as Akamai's largest customer is TikTok. The company signed a roughly $100M cloud infrastructure services deal with TikTok, but ongoing geopolitical risks and the potential for a TikTok ban may weigh on sentiment.

Nomura downgraded Baidu (NASDAQ:BIDU) to Neutral from Buy, saying that the company has a good chance to win the race in AI search, but the potential costs of winning could be "formidable."

BIDU has a PT of $98, cut from $104. "Baidu’s Ernie Bot, once a leader among Chinese LLMs thanks to its first-mover advantage and Baidu’s years of investments into its AI capabilities, seems to have lost its luster," the analysts said.

STMicroelectronics (NYSE:STM) was upgraded to Buy from Hold by Jefferies due to expectations of revenue growth accelerating in the second half of 2025 and higher content in Apple's (AAPL) upcoming iPhone 17. Jefferies also increased PT on the stock to €34 from €23, or $35 from $24.

NXP Semiconductors (NASDAQ:NXPI) was upgraded by Citi to Buy, as Analog Devices' (ADI) most recent quarterly results suggest an "imminent" recovery in the analog part of the semiconductor market.

Morgan Stanley downgraded Workday (NASDAQ:WDAY) to Equal-Weight from Overweight and lowered PT to $275 from $330, amid concerns that recent investments have failed to spark top-line growth.

data.title: Berkshire operating earnings reach nearly $50B in 2024, cash pile hits another record
data.description:
Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.A)(NYSE:BRK.B) on Saturday reported a surge in Q4 operating earnings, helping the sprawling conglomerate deliver an overall 27% jump in full-year 2024 operating earnings to $47.44B.

The company said Q4 operating earnings increased 71.3% Y/Y to $14.53B. That was its best bottom-line quarterly performance for 2024, compared to a 6.2% fall in Q3, a 15.5% gain in Q2, and a 39.1% rise in Q1.

Overall full-year net earnings attributable to shareholders fell 7.5% Y/Y to $89B in 2024 from $96.22B in 2023.

The conglomerate used about $2.9B to buy back its shares during 2024. Its cash pile swelled to a record $334.20B as of December 31, 2024, from $325.21B as of September 30, 2024.

See below for a breakdown of the company's full-year earnings performance by segment:

The Omaha, Neb.-based giant's insurance and energy businesses were its best performers in 2024, while its railroad operations and its manufacturing, service and retailing segment experienced softness.

Berkshire's (NYSE:BRK.A)(NYSE:BRK.B) insurance business is broken down into underwriting and investing. Underwriting decisions are the responsibility of unit managers, while investing actions are handled by famed top boss Warren Buffett.

Its full-year 2024 insurance underwriting after-tax earnings soared 66% Y/Y, driven by strong results at insurer GEICO, and estimated claims of $1.2B from hurricanes Helene and Milton.

Taking a closer look at GEICO, the insurer wrote premiums worth $42.92B (+7.7% Y/Y) in 2024 and earned premiums worth $42.25B (+7.6% Y/Y). Pre-tax underwriting earnings more than doubled to $7.81B.

Berkshire's (NYSE:BRK.A)(NYSE:BRK.B) investment gains, most of which are unrealized, came in at $41.56B in 2024, compared to investment gains of $58.87B in 2023.

Turning to the full-year performance of the conglomerate's railroad business, after-tax earnings at BNSF slipped 1.1% Y/Y to $5.03B in 2024.

BNSF operates one of the largest railroad systems in North America, with over 32.5K route miles of track in 28 states. Its car/unit volumes gained 6.5% for the full-year, driven by a double-digit surge in freight volume of consumer products and a single-digit rise in volume of agricultural products.

Despite the rise in volumes, BNSF's 2024 earnings were hit by a $290M charge related to a labor agreement that was finalized in Q4 and by increased litigation accruals.

Looking at Berkshire Hathaway Energy, after-tax earnings increased 60% Y/Y to $3.73B in 2024. The gain "reflected lower estimated wildfire loss accruals at PacifiCorp and higher earnings from natural gas pipelines, primarily due to reductions in earnings attributable to noncontrolling interests, and other energy businesses."

data.title: Defensive sectors flashing caution sign with recent market outperformance, BofA says
data.description:
The defensive Consumer Staples and Health Care sectors have recently performed better than other areas of the stock market, which is a cautionary signal about the U.S. economy, according to Bank of America.

The outperformance of defensive and bond-sensitive stocks is starting to flag a pullback in economic activity, Bank of America ((BofA)) said Friday. Such as economic shift could stoke a “disruption” in the equity bull market, it said.

The S&P 500 (SP500) has lost 0.6% over the past month, as of Friday, while the Consumer Staples (NYSEARCA:XLP) and Health Care (NYSEARCA:XLV) sectors have risen 6.8% and 2.6%, respectively. Those two groups have been the benchmark’s (SP500) top-performing over the past month.

There’s risk of an “unanticipated slowdown in growth on housing, tailwinds of wealth effect & jobs growth tailing off, inflation nagging consumer confidence, [and] US government heading into recession,” BofA Strategist Michael Hartnett said in its Flow Show note.

A 65% rise in government discretionary spending in the past five years is a “big reason why” U.S. nominal GDP has increased +50%, Hartnett said. The Trump administration is currently pursuing massive spending cuts, which could include defense reductions.

Slowdown risk is bigger than that of inflation heating up and snapping stocks out of a trading range, as U.S. President Donald Trump is “unlikely to provoke” inflation through big tariffs and immigration cuts in H1, Hartnett said.

The Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR ETF (NYSEARCA:XLP) has gained 5.9% over the past month, partially from moves by its top five holdings:

  • Costco (COST): +9.2%
  • Walmart (WMT): +1.8%
  • Procter & Gamble (PG): +5.3%
  • Coca-Cola (KO): +14%
  • Philip Morris International (PM): +24.7%

Investors can track the S&P 500 (SP500) via ETFs such as (VOO), (UPRO), (SPY), (IVV), and (SH).

data.title: Warren Buffett highlights Berkshire Hathaway's status as the largest U.S. corporate taxpayer
data.description:
Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.B) (NYSE:BRK.A) paid $26.8B to the Internal Revenue Service last year, "about 5% of what all of corporate America paid," Chairman, CEO and legendary investor Warren Buffett wrote in his annual letter to shareholders on Saturday.

He recounted the company's turnaround, from a failing textiles company when he took control of the firm 60 years ago, to a thriving insurance and investing behemoth. That success has enabled the company to pay those taxes to the American government as well as "sizable amounts for income taxes to foreign governments and to 44 states," he said.

"Cash income-tax payments to the U.S.Treasury, miniscule in the first decade, now aggregate more than $101 billion ... and counting."

Once again, Buffett said Berkshire (NYSE:BRK.B) wouldn't have succeeded as well as it has in any other country than America.

"So thank you, Uncle Sam," he wrote. "Someday, your nieces and nephews at Berkshire hope to send you even larger payments than we did in 2024. Spend it wisely. Take care of the many who, for no fault of their own, get the short straws in life."

Then he appeals to government leaders to take care of the country's and business's well-being. "And never forget that we need you to maintain a stable currency, and that result requires both wisdom and vigilance on your part," Buffett wrote.

Expect the Oracle of Omaha to expound further on the theme at the company's annual meeting on May 3, to be held from 10:30 AM CT to 1:00 PM, shorter than previous meetings.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

  • SEC filing.

data.title: Apple's iPhone 16 now boasts five models with 16e release
data.description:
Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) releases the iPhone 16e on February 28, giving consumers five iPhone 16 models to choose from.

The expanded list of models features a wide price range, with the 16e starting at $599 and the 1TB Pro Max going for $1,599. Below is a breakdown of the features and pricing for each model.

iPhone 16

It measures 5.81 by 2.82 by 0.31 inches and weighs 6 ounces. The display is 6.1 inches with a resolution of 2,556 by 1,179 pixels. It has an aluminum frame and three cameras, 48 and 12 megapixel rear cameras and a 12MP front camera. It features Apple's A18 processor. It starts at $799 for 128GB of storage, $899 for 256BG and $1,099 for 512GB.

iPhone 16 Plus

The next level up measures 6.33 by 3.06 by 0.31 inches and weighs 7.03 ounces. The display measures 6.7 inches with a resolution of 2,796 by 1,290 pixels. It has an aluminum frame and the same cameras as the base model. It also has the same A18 processor as the base model. It starts at $899 for the 128GB, $999 for 256GB and $1,199 for 512GB.

iPhone 16 Pro

The next tier measures 5.89 by 2.81 by 0.32 inches and weighs 7.03 ounces. The display is 6.3 inches with 2,622 by 1,206 pixel resolution. It features a titanium frame and has one additional 48MP rear camera. It features the A18 Pro, which has six GPU cores over the five GPU cores in the A18. The A18 Pro processor performs better for applications such as gaming and media creation. It cost $999 for 128GB, $1,099 for 256GB, $1,299 for 512GB and $1,499 for 1TB.

iPhone 16 Pro Max

The top-of-the-line model measures 6.42 by 3.06 by 0.32 inches and weighs 8 ounces. The display is 6.9 inches, which is the largest screen ever for an iPhone, with 2,868 by 1,320 pixels. It also has a titanium frame and the same cameras as the Pro, and is powered by the A18 Pro processor. It starts at $1,199 for 256GB, $1,399 for 512GB and $1,599 for 1TB.

iPhone 16e

The latest in the series is also its most budget friendly, starting at $599 for 128GB, $699 for 256GB and $899 for 512GB. It measures 5.78 by 2.82 by 0.31 inches and weighs 5.88 ounces. The display is 6.1 inches with a resolution of 2,532 by 1,170. It has an aluminum frame and a 48MP rear camera and a 12MP front camera. It has an Apple A18 processor and features Apple's in-house modem chip, known as the C1.

During the fourth quarter of 2024, the Pro Max accounted for the bulk of iPhone sales at 23%, according to data by CIRP. The iPhone 16 base model was next at 21% followed by the 16 Pro at 16% and the Plus at 8%. Older models constituted the rest, led by the iPhone 14 at 16%.

The highlight of all the iPhone 16 models are their ability to utilize Apple Intelligence, which the company defines as "the personal intelligence system that harnesses the power of Apple silicon to understand and create language and images, take action across apps, and draw from personal context to simplify and accelerate everyday tasks while taking an extraordinary step forward for privacy in AI."

data.title: Insider trades: JPMorgan, Bristol-Myers Squibb among notable names this week
data.description:
A weekly summary of insider trading highlighting a few notable buying and selling by investors, directors and executives. The trades have taken place between February 18, 2025 and February 21, 2025.

• JPMorgan Chase (NYSE:JPM) disclosed a series of transactions this week. Chairman and Chief Executive Officer Jamie Dimon sold 866,361 shares at a price of $269.84 per share for a total value of $233M. Chief Information Officer Lori Beer sold 2,053 shares for $554,044, Head of Human Resources, Robin Leopold, sold 1,250 shares for a value of $337,600 and Co-CEO CIB Troy Rohrbaugh sold 37,500 shares, totaling $10M in value.

• Kinder Morgan (NYSE:KMI) disclosed a transaction by its Director Michael Morgan, who sold 160,000 shares of common stock at a price of $26.85 per share, totaling $4.3M. Morgan now holds about 285,087 shares of the company.

• Samit Hirawat, EVP, Chief Medical Officer. Drug Development at Bristol-Myers Squibb (NYSE:BMY) bought 1,823 shares at a price of $54.84 per share for a total value of $100,000. Hirawat now holds 63,932 shares of the company.

• Michael Mahoney, Director at CVS Health (NYSE:CVS) disclosed a transaction this week. Mahoney purchased 30,000 shares at a price of $66.7 per share for a total value of $2M. Mahoney now holds 39,356 shares of the company.

• John G. Rice, Director at American International Group (NYSE:AIG) bought 10,000 shares at a price of $77 per share for a total value of $770,000. Rice now holds 10,000 shares of the company.

data.title: Crude oil drops to YTD low as OPEC expected to delay planned production hike
data.description:
Crude oil futures closed Friday at their lowest settlement price this year, with both WTI and Brent benchmarks tumbling more than $2/bbl, fueled by near-term supply concerns and expectations that OPEC+ will postpone returning barrels to the market in April as planned.

Oil traders and analysts expect OPEC+ will again delay plans to revive oil production, despite pressure from President Trump, as global markets contend with a potential surplus; a postponement of the group's planned 120K bbl/day output increase would mark the fourth delay in plans to revive production that was first halted in 2022.

"The downside for prices is high if OPEC proceeds with production hikes," ANZ Research wrote, as the cartel and its allies face potential challenges from shifting U.S. policy and risks of a trade war, while renewed maximum pressure on Iran, sanctions on Russia and the halt of Venezuelan crude oil purchases add to the complexity, and rising fiscal pressures among OPEC members adds to their focus on maximizing revenue.

"Given prices in the mid-$70s, we continue to anticipate that [OPEC+] postpones the beginning of bringing back withheld oil supply to market," according to Citigroup analysts, adding that a decision to bring back more oil to market "might only come if the U.S. exerts more sanctions pressure on Iran amid potential negotiations."

Reuters reported the Trump administration is piling pressure on Iraq to allow oil exports from the Kurdistan region to restart or face sanctions, as a speedy resumption of exports that has cut flows of more than 300K bbl/day for nearly two years would help offset a potential drop in Iranian oil exports, which Trump has pledged to cut to zero.

Analysts said the relative calm in the Middle East as the Gaza ceasefire held has reduced risk in the market, and pointed to media reports indicating that researchers at the Wuhan Institute of Virology in China said they discovered a new coronavirus in bats.

Investors also continued to evaluate another increase in U.S. crude oil inventories, reported on Thursday, as seasonal maintenance at refineries led to lower processing, the Energy Information Administration said.

Traders also are watching U.S. efforts for talks to end the Russia-Ukraine war, as "an array of geopolitical, military and macroeconomic crosscurrents continues to limit upside follow-through in WTI to above the $73.50 level while restricting downside to the $70 area," Ritterbusch wrote.

Russia said oil flows from the Caspian Pipeline Consortium, a major route for crude exports from Kazakhstan through Russia, were cut by 30%-40% earlier this week after a Ukrainian drone attack on a pumping station, but industry sources told Reuters that Kazakhstan is pumping record high oil volumes despite the damage to the CPC export route.

Friday's losses pushed both major crude oil benchmarks into the red for the week, with front-month April Nymex crude (CL1:COM) and April Brent crude (CO1:COM) both finishing -0.4% to $70.40/bbl and $74.43/bbl, respectively; on Friday, WTI fell 2.9% and Brent dropped 2.7%.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG)

U.S. natural gas futures ended +13.6% for the week as an Arctic blast lifted heating demand across much of the country, while deliveries to liquefied natural gas export terminals hit new highs above 16B cf/day; front-month Nymex natural gas (NG1:COM) for March delivery gained 2% on Friday to $4.234/MMBtu.

Cheniere Energy's announcement this week of a first cargo from its Corpus Christi Stage 3 expansion was "big news for LNG," Gelber & Associates said, and while milder temperatures next week are expected to reduce natural gas demand, and production is seen picking up after freeze-offs, prices should find support in the growing storage deficit given this winter’s hefty withdrawals.

Energy stocks, as represented by the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE), ended the week +0.9%.

Top 5 gainers in energy and natural resources in the past 5 days: Energy Focus (EFOI) +65.3%, Solaris Energy Infrastructure (SEI) +31.9%, SolarEdge Technologies (SEDG) +18.6%, San Juan Basin Royalty Trust (SJT) +15.6%, Obsidian Energy (OBE) +12.8%.

Top 10 decliners in energy and natural resources in the past 5 days: Critical Minerals (CRML) -41.8%, Perpetua Resources (PPTA) -30.2%, Oklo (OKLO) -29.5%, ESS Tech (GWH) -26.2%, Lightbridge (LTBR) -25.5%, Coeur Mining (CDE) -24.4%, Nuscale Power (SMR) -22.9%, Hudbay Minerals (HBM) -21.7%, Uranium Energy (UEC) -18.6%, Hecla Mining (HL) -18%.

Source: Barchart.com
